7. MST

A spanning tree of a graph is a subgraph that includes all vertices and is a tree (exactly ) edges. Adding a non-tree edge to creates a unique cycle called the fundamental cycle of that edge.

Propositions

  • If you add a non-tree edge and remove any edge on its fundamental cycle , you still get a spanning tree.
  • The number of trees on labeled vertices is (Cayley’s formula).
  • If a graph has distinct edge weights, then its MST is unique.
  • In any cycle, the heaviest edge is not in some MST.

Proposition 2 (MST Properties). See crib sheet.

  • Edge-count by weight is invariant
  • Cut property: For any cut (S, V \ S), the lightest edge crossing that cut belongs to every MST.
  • Cycle property: For any cycle, the heaviest edge on that cycle belongs to no MST. Also, every non-tree edge is at least as heavy as every edge on its fundamental cycle.
  • Exchange. Let and be different MSTs. For any , there exists such that both and are both MSTs.
Proof

Cycle property: assume for contradiction there exists a cycle with strictly maximal edge and for some MST. Consider it’s fundamental cut. There must be an edge that crosses the cut, and has strictly smaller weight, contradicting minimality.

□

It turns out the converse of the cycle property is true: suppose is in no minimum spanning tree of . Run Kruskal with first out of edges of weight . Then there must be a path using edges of weight smaller than . Alternatively, just consider the fundamental cycle of . For more discussion, see Quiz 1 3b from 2025 Spring.

7.1. Motivating a Greedy Algorithm

We attempt to construct an MST using by building it up from , adding “safe” edges that enforce the invariant that is in some MST.

  • A cut respects if no edge of crosses the cut. Note that edges of can lie on both sides of the cut.
  • Say an edge crossing a cut is a light edge if it has minimum weight out of all edges crossing the cut.

Theorem: Let be any set of edges of that is in some MST. Let be any cut that respects . Let be a light edge for the cut. Then is also in some MST, so is a safe edge.

Proof

Let be a MST containing . Let be a light edge connecting and . Let be the path between and in . It must contain an edge crossing the cut. By the swap property, is still a spanning tree, with weight

(16)

Hence T’ is also an MST and is part of an MST.

□

We invoke the theorem times to construct an MST. A nontrivial cut respecting always exists since does not connect all vertices in . We can take to be any CC of .

7.2. Kruskal’s Algorithm

Kruskal’s algorithm: Let our subgraph be a forest of spanning trees (starting as all singletons). At each step, we pick an edge of minimum weight that connects two of the trees and merge the trees. This edge is a light edge for the cut separating one of the trees from the rest.

Implementation:

  • Start with an empty tree . Initialize union-find data structure on the vertices, and sort the edges in non-decreasing order.
  • For all in sorted order, if and are in different trees then take the union of those trees and add the edge to .
  • Return .

Let and . The runtime is worst case . This simplies to since .

Remark. Kruskal can also be used to construct a spanning forest. Prim cannot.

7.3. Prim’s Algorithm

Prim’s algorithm: Grow one big tree and append the singletons one by one. Take to be the min weight edge connecting to an isolated vertex (“next cheapest vertex to add”).

Implementation (analogous to Dijkstra):

  • Start with an empty tree . Assign each vertex a key recording the cheapest cost to connect to the growing tree. Also record a parent array.
  • Set and for all other vertices.
  • Maintain a decrease-key min priority queue on the vertices.
  • While the queue is not empty, add the cheapest vertex to the tree and update the parent array. Relax all neighbors of the cheapest vertex that are still in . That way, the priority queue is always finding the “next cheapest vertex to add”.

Runtime:

Remarks

  • Krustal’s runs faster if the edges are already sorted or can be sorted in linear time (e.g. radix sort).
  • Chazelle 2000 runs in .
  • Karger-Klein-Tarjan 1993 is a randomized algoirhtm that runs in expected time.
  • Pettie and Ramachandran 2002 is an optimal algorithm but we can’t analyze running time.
